post: The Constitution gives Congress the power to declare war. It also makes the President Commander-in-Chief of the armed forces. For 235 years, those 2 facts have been in tension, and every time America has faced a military crisis, the question resurfaces: who decides? The War Powers Act of 1973 tried to answer that. It requires the President to notify Congress within 48 hours of deploying troops, and to withdraw them within 60 days unless Congress authorizes continued action. Every President since Nixon has questioned whether the law is even constitutional. Congress has rarely enforced it. So where does that leave us? BRI's latest blog breaks down the history of presidential war powers – from the Constitutional Convention of 1787 to the post-9/11 authorizations – with links to free classroom resources that help students engage in this debate themselves. Because understanding who holds power, and why it matters, is exactly what civics education is for 👇  https://bit.ly/4vWdqjH  #WarPowersAct  #BRI  #Constitution

In 1864, the United States held a free election in the middle of a civil war. Lincoln ran. Lincoln won. And on March 4, 1865, he stood before a country still at war and chose to speak not of vengeance, but of binding up wounds, honoring sacrifice, and pursuing a just and lasting peace. That commitment to democracy, even under the most extreme pressure, is the founding ideal the Second Inaugural embodies. BRI's Primary Source Essentials covers the essential meaning of this address in just a few minutes, perfect for the classroom or a quick, substantive watch. Watch it here:  https://bit.ly/3NaVq3D  #AbrahamLincoln  #AmericanHistory  #CivicEducation  #TeachingHistory  #Democracy

"Participating in this program changed the way I approach disagreement." That's Arianna Oropeza, a high school student from Miami, FL, one of the finalists going to London this November to debate her UK peers. BRI is proud to announce the 12 US finalists selected for Debating Matters: Transatlantic, a 5-day program in London this November where American high schoolers will join UK students to debate some of the most pressing issues of our time: artificial intelligence, digital surveillance, NATO, and free speech. These 12 were chosen from 50 students nationwide through a rigorous process of coaching sessions, practice debates, peer feedback, and a live qualifier. Civil discourse is a skill. These students are proof it can be learned. Thank you to Carnegie Corporation of New York for the generous investment that made this program possible. Meet the finalists:  https://bit.ly/4cXA3LY  #CarnegieCorporation  #BRI  #Debate  #CivilDiscourse

2 weeks left in thisCivic Star Challenge cycle. Here's what this has been about from the start: capturing what civic education looks like in America's 250th year. The Declaration isn't a relic. Liberty isn't abstract. And somewhere in your classroom, that's been demonstrated. That story belongs on the record. Share it before May 15. Enter the weekly $300 drawing. And help close out this historic school year the right way. Final entries here:  https://bit.ly/463KXxf  #CivicStarChallenge  #America250  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeacher  #BRI

The MyImpact Challenge closes in 5 days: May 3 at 11:59 PM PT. You already know whether you have students who qualify. You already know their project is real. The only question left is whether they submit it before the window closes, or let this be the year their work went unrecognized. A student in Austin, Texas built an accessible tennis program for children with disabilities. He almost didn't submit because he didn't think it was good enough. He won $10,000. Students from Fort Washington, Pennsylvania founded a youth-led policy think tank. Students in Hackensack, New Jersey built an environmental restoration nonprofit. Students in McLean, Virginia launched an initiative that's still growing. Every one of them submitted. The students who don't submit by May 3 will carry that question longer than they expect. If there's a teacher in your network who needs this reminder, send this to them. If there are students still on the fence, this is the moment. $40,000 in prizes. Open to students 13-19 across the country. 5 days. 👉  https://bit.ly/3fyGGr3  #MyImpactChallenge  #CivicsEducation  #FinalCall  #BRI

The people shaping civics in the Seattle region will be in the same room on April 30. We've brought together educators, civic leaders, and technology partners for a forum at MOHAI built around real conversation and collaborative problem-solving. If you work at the intersection of education, technology, or community then this is your room. Six days out. A few seats remain. April 30 | 6:30-9:30 PM | Museum of History and Industry, Seattle Register here: 🔗  https://bit.ly/3OOH0He  #CivicEducation  #Civics  #EdTech  #Seattle  #MOHAI  #CivicsInnovation  #BillOfRightsInstitute

Thomas Jefferson believed in natural rights, civic virtue, and self-government more eloquently than almost anyone in American history. He also knew slavery was wrong, and spent his life rationalizing why he couldn't act on that knowledge. What do we do with a founder who articulated the ideals of a free society but failed to live up to them? Historian Andrew Burstein argues we start by understanding before we judge. In this episode of BRI Scholar Talks, Burstein joins Tony Williams to examine the character behind the Declaration: the intellectual brilliance, the moral contradictions, and the enduring questions Jefferson's legacy leaves for every generation. Watch it here:  https://bit.ly/4tpRYBU  #AmericanHistory  #CivicEducation  #TeachingHistory  #SocialStudies  #BRI
